translatedText: November 24, 1632
Born into a Portuguese Jewish family in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
Inherited his father's import business at the age of 21 upon his father's death.
Around 1656
Expelled from the Jewish community with a writ of cherem (excommunication) under the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote the 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy' in Rijnsburg.
Befriended Johan de Witt, the political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published the 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
The 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned by the Dutch government.
Began writing the 'Tractatus Politicus'.
February 21, 1675
Died of tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work, 'Ethics', though unpublished during his lifetime, and the first edition of the 'Tractatus Politicus' was published posthumously.
Authorship of 'Ethics'

Incorporated Descartes' foundationalism partially while opposing mind-body dualism, embedding this philosophical stance into his magnum opus 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.
Synthesis of natural substance and moral essence
Revived ancient Stoic thought in a modern sense, undertaking a philosophical synthesis of natural substance and moral essence.
Presentation of the concept of Conatus
Proposed the concept of 'Conatus' as the inherent desire or effort to preserve, develop, and perfect one's being, viewing its full expression as happiness.
Establishment of monistic pantheism
Viewing God and Nature as two names for the same substance, he established a monistic and pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic inclinations.
Contributions to modern rationalist philosophy
Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe, laying the groundwork for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.
How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?

Although well-versed in Jewish culture, his critical ideas led to his expulsion from the Jewish community at the age of 23, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.
What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
He advocated a monistic pantheism that considered God and Nature as two names for the same substance, and proposed the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect one's being.
What profession did Spinoza have to support himself?
He earned a meager living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including a professorship, during his lifetime.
What are Spinoza's representative works?
His life's work is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and his other works include the 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and the 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.
Is the saying, 'Even if I knew that tomorrow the world would go to pieces, I would still plant my apple tree', a quote from Spinoza?
This quote is attributed to Martin Luther, and the original text notes that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.
Around 545 BC
Birth of Sun Tzu (estimated)
515 BC
Invited by King Helü of Wu on the recommendation of Wu Zixu, becoming a military strategist for the State of Wu
506 BC
Launched an expedition against the State of Chu with Helü and Wu Zixu as commanders, capturing the Chu capital, Ying
496 BC
Despite Sun Tzu's objections, Helü attacked the State of Yue, was defeated, and died
Around 470 BC
Death of Sun Tzu (estimated)
Authorship of The Art of War
As a strategist during the Spring and Autumn period, he authored the 13 chapters of 'The Art of War'.
Appointment as Wu's military strategist
On Wu Zixu's recommendation, he became the military strategist for King Helü of Wu and trained the army.
Victory in the Chu expedition
In 506 BC, he commanded the expedition against Chu with Helü, contributing to the capture of the capital, Ying.
Assistance in the great victory over Yue
Assisted Helü's successor, Fuchai, in building national strength and subsequently contributed to a major victory by attacking the State of Yue.
Who is Sun Tzu?
Sun Tzu was a military strategist in the Spring and Autumn period. His courtesy name was Changqing, and he is better known by the honorific Sun Tzu. He authored 'The Art of War'.
Are the records of Sun Tzu's life clear?
Although there are important records such as Sima Qian's 'Records of the Grand Historian', details about his life are subject to debate, and his life after retirement remains unknown.
How did Sun Tzu become a military strategist for the State of Wu?
Fleeing with his clan to the State of Wu, he came to know the chancellor Wu Zixu, and upon Wu Zixu's recommendation, he was invited by King Helü of Wu to become a military strategist.
What is Sun Tzu's representative work?
Sun Tzu's representative work is 'The Art of War', which has long been treated as an important military treatise in the Sinosphere.
April 26, 1711
Born in Chirnside, north of Edinburgh.
Changed his surname from Home to Hume, and worked as a clerk in Bristol for several months.
Completed his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature', at the age of 26.
Applied for a professorship at the University of Edinburgh with a treatise on morals and politics but was rejected.
Served as a tutor for the Marquess of Annandale and began writing 'The History of England'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals'.
Returned to Edinburgh and served as the Keeper of the Advocates' Library.
Published 'The History of England' in six volumes, written over 15 years.
Published 'Four Dissertations' and 'The Natural History of Religion'.
Published 'Sister Peg'.
Worked as a secretary to the British Ambassador in France and befriended Rousseau.
Published 'My Own Life', died on August 25, and was buried in Edinburgh.
His 'Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion' was published posthumously.
Role as a philosopher, economist, and historian
A Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian, and one of the most prominent figures in Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.
Authorship of 'A Treatise of Human Nature'
Wrote his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature', at age 26, which is considered one of the most important books in the history of philosophy today.
Authorship of 'The History of England'
Wrote 'The History of England' over 15 years and published it in a total of six volumes between 1754 and 1762.
Raising the 'Is-Ought Problem'
Raised the 'is-ought problem', arguing that descriptive statements of 'is' alone cannot lead to prescriptive conclusions of 'ought', which became a core issue in modern meta-ethics.
Proposing 'Sympathy' as the psychological foundation of morality
Viewed morality as a matter of sentiment and established 'sympathy' as the psychological foundation of morality, becoming a forerunner to utilitarian ethics.
Advocating an empirical methodology for the science of man
Advocated the science of man to be studied through empirical methods of 'experience and observation', attempting to analyze the basic facts of the human mind.
Raising the Problem of Induction
Raised the 'problem of induction', arguing that the reliability of inductive reasoning cannot be guaranteed.
Skeptical stance on causality
Cast doubt on the problem of causality by viewing causality as merely a probability inductively established by habit.
Elucidating the role of money in a market economy
Argued that money is merely a medium of exchange in the market system, and that in a society reaching equilibrium, the quantity of money is of no consequence.
Who was David Hume?
David Hume was a Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian, and one of the most prominent figures associated with Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.
What are the characteristics of Hume's philosophy?
Hume's philosophy is generally seen as an extension of skepticism, and there is debate over whether naturalistic elements are treated as significantly important.
What stance did Hume take on religion?
Hume expressed his views on all religions, but as commenting on religion was risky in his time, his religion-related works were published posthumously.
What were Hume's political views?
Hume belonged to the skeptical yet moderate Tories, emphasizing the importance of the rule of law and public spirit for the community.
How did Hume view the economy?
Hume believed that commerce and trade raised people's living standards and brought cultural refinement, and that the dictatorial power of a government would weaken as its citizens became wealthier.
August 29, 1632
Born as the son of a legal professional in Wrington, Somerset.
Entered Westminster School and graduated with excellent grades.
Entered Christ Church, Oxford, as a scholarship student and discovered Cartesian philosophy.
Received his Bachelor of Arts.
Began a 5-year stint as a tutor at Oxford University.
Around 1664
Began studying natural sciences and medicine.
Served as a secretary to a diplomatic mission in Brandenburg, Germany, witnessing the possibility of religious tolerance.
Appointed secretary to the Board of Trade and Plantations.
Began writing his major work, 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'.
Returned to England.
Fled to the Netherlands to escape the Royalists.
Returned home following the accession of William III due to the Glorious Revolution.
Became Commissioner of Appeals and gained fame by publishing 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', written during his exile.
Retired to Essex.
